sections.Limits of the Frame: This entry is in review after initial commentary analysis. The frame is medium because the chapter is source-layer dense and the claims are high-risk; historical Jesus, Gnostic materials, original sin, and blame narratives need final review before stronger weighting.

sections.Where This Sits: This chapter sits at the transition from Jewish messianic background into Christian reinterpretation. Its central work is source separation: historical Jesus, biblical Jesus, Gnostic Jesus, Roman execution, and later theological memory are not the same evidentiary layer.

title: Civilization #24: Resurrecting the Gnostic Jesus

title: Civilization #25: Paul of Tarsus, Messiah of Rome

title: Civilization #26: Constantine's Monotheistic Revolution

title: Civilization #27: Augustine's Empire of God

title: Civilization #29: Dante's Divine Comedy and the Liberation of the Human Imagination

cross_volume_corridor: This unit is part of the [Homer to Dante corridor](../corridors/homer-to-dante.md). Use that corridor to compare Dante's replacement of older epic authority with Great Books close reading and the later Renaissance return.

sections.Historical Pressure Points:

title: Civilization #30: Dante as the Second Coming of Homer

sections.Historical Pressure Points:

title: Civilization #31: The Oceanic Currents of History

sections.Historical Pressure Points:

sections.Return Path: Return here when later chapters invoke Rome as political inheritance, imperial warning, or American mirror. Use the commentary to separate Roman source claims, modern analogy, and explicit prediction.
sections.Where This Sits: This chapter applies the second-semester model to Rome and then uses Rome to frame America. It sits at the hinge between classical inheritance and present political analogy: Rome becomes both an ancient case and a warning structure for modern imperial power.

title: Civilization #32: Rome's Rise, Fall, and Legacy

sections.Historical Pressure Points:

title: Civilization #33: The Rise and Fall of the Byzantine Empire

sections.Historical Pressure Points:

title: Civilization #34: The Useful Fiction of the Holy Roman Empire

title: Civilization #36: Memory of the Norse

sections.Return Path: Return here when later chapters discuss Robespierre, Napoleon, revolution, nationalism, civil religion, Enlightenment reason, political sacrifice, or the replacement of traditional religious authority by the nation-state.

title: Civilization #46: The Revolution of Reason

title: Civilization #47: The Passion of Robespierre

sections.Historical Pressure Points:

sections.Reading Posture: Read it as a strong transition from revolutionary purity to imperial organization. The chapter is not simply about Napoleon as a great man; it is about the conditions that let a new political-military figure gather the Revolution into a durable myth.
sections.Return Path: Return here when later chapters discuss empire, nationalism, charisma, talent systems, post-revolutionary order, political myth, or the relation between French Revolution energy and modern imperial statecraft.
sections.Where This Sits: This chapter closes the French Revolution trilogy by showing Napoleon as the figure who turns revolutionary disruption into imperial myth. The lecture links Robespierre, talent promotion, military charisma, nationalism, and empire into one post-revolutionary settlement.

title: Civilization #48: Napoleon's Empire of Myth

sections.Return Path: Return here when later chapters discuss Britain, America, capitalism, corporations, middle-class culture, domesticity, art, hypocrisy, trade, or the commercial roots of Anglo-American power.

The Dutch Golden Age becomes a bridge from early modern trade and capitalism into middle-class life, art, domesticity, and later imperial habits.

title: Civilization #49: The Dutch Golden Age and the Rise of the Middle Class

sections.Return Path: Return here when later chapters discuss Shakespeare, America, Anglo-American empire, maritime power, constitutionalism, Protestant inheritance, imperial competition, or the transfer of British conflicts into American politics.
